<!-- GENERATED by cc-guides from .claude/fragments/CLAUDE.md/ ‚Äî do not edit; edit the fragments and run 'cc-guides render'. -->
@AGENTS.md

## Claude-Specific Rules

- **Clarify via `AskUserQuestion`, never inline prose** (¬ß Ask Before Assuming) ‚Äî concrete picks, up to 4 questions per call, batched.

## Task Tracking

Non-trivial work flows `pending` ‚Üí `in_progress` ‚Üí `completed`: `TaskCreate` before starting, `TaskUpdate` as you go. The task list is the source of truth ‚Äî complete or explicitly defer every task before stopping.

## Durable Records (cc-notes)

Durable knowledge routes through cc-notes, never loose markdown in the tree ‚Äî via the MCP tools first, the `cc-notes` CLI only when no server is live: a decision or durable fact ‚Üí `note_add`; living guidance with a `when` read-trigger ‚Üí `doc_add`; an append-only chronology with artifacts ‚Üí `log_add` / `log_append` (with `attach`); friction ‚Üí `papercut`; a repeatable procedure ‚Üí `runbook_add`; orient with `status`. Decision memos, gate verdicts, handoffs, and status write-ups are notes/docs ‚Äî not `*-memo.md` files committed alongside results. The tree carries only published docs and source.

## Plan Execution & Orchestration

Plans you author must specify, and plans you execute must enforce, that substantive work runs as **dynamic workflows** (`Workflow` tool): the script holds the loop, branching, and intermediate results; your context holds only final answers. This section is standing authorization to invoke `Workflow`. Multi-phase work runs as workflows in sequence (understand ‚Üí implement ‚Üí verify); read each result before dispatching the next.

Exceptions: trivial single-file edits, single file reads, and single targeted `ccx`/`LSP`/`Grep` lookups stay at the main-agent level; a lone ad-hoc investigation gets one subagent (fallbacks: AGENTS.md `## Parallelize Independent Work`).

**Sweeps are workflows, not teams.** A bounded N-unit sweep ‚Äî test-module conversions, per-file migrations, mechanical refactor passes ‚Äî runs as a `Workflow` fan-out, never as `Agent` calls nested under a teammate: a teammate's own subagents are foreground-only ([teams limitations](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams#limitations)), so the teammate sits blocked while they run, and the official sizing puts teams at a handful of long-running peers vs workflows at dozens-to-hundreds of agents per run ([workflows](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/workflows#when-to-use-a-workflow)). A teammate that finds itself holding a sweep delegates it to a workflow.

**Docs & prose edits.** A routine documentation or prose change ‚Äî Markdown, READMEs, changelogs, doc-site pages, `.md` guide fragments, code comments; regardless of file count ‚Äî needn't run as a dynamic workflow or carry an adversarial-verify pass; make the edit directly. This drops *orchestration weight only*: still route writing per the **Models** table (never down-route prose), still give any plan its `## Workflow Plan` line, and still run the prose bar (`writing-docs` + `slop-cop`) on substantive prose.

**Quality patterns**: pick per task ‚Äî adversarial verify, judge panel, loop-until-dry, multi-modal sweep. Reviews and audits lean thorough; quick checks lean brief.

**Models** ‚Äî route per agent, up-front by task type. Higher = better; cost = cheaper:

| Model | Cost | Int | Taste | Route here |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| fable-5 | 2 | 9 | 9 | Orchestration, design/architecture review, hard planning, all prose/writing (docs, READMEs, release notes, any user-facing text ‚Äî never down-route writing), very sensitive or error-prone implementation, and the synthesis/accept-reject pass over review findings. The escalation target for every other lane. |
| opus-4.8 | 4 | 8 | 8 | Default ‚Äî when in doubt, opus; ~2x cheaper than fable and nearly as capable. Implementation stays here at `xhigh` when the path is genuinely undecided ‚Äî ambiguous, exploratory, long-horizon, decision-dense, or large net-new work whose mid-flight judgment calls pile up (design micro-decisions, adaptive tool-driving, a plan that emerges while coding), even when the scope is nominally bounded: delegate it rather than editing inline on fable ‚Äî sustained hands-on tool-driving (browser automation, QA sweeps, bulk extract/fill/snapshot) delegates the same way, not just code edits; a single gated go/no-go interaction can stay inline. Decompose before defaulting here: implementation that splits into bounded, decision-light units fans out to gpt-5.6-sol (see that row) instead of running as one long opus agent ‚Äî opus spends ~2x the tier-median output tokens per task (AA 2026-07-14; measured same day, implementing opus subagents put 73‚Äì86% of wall-clock into generation, roughly half of it thinking). |
| sonnet-5 | 8 | 6 | 6 | The low-effort wrapper for codex runs, and the recon-lane carve-out. Recon and routine subagent work ‚Äî enumerations, config/wiring locates, subsystem traces, pattern sweeps, and routine chores (diff and log triage, classify, extract, digest, doc lookup) ‚Äî now default to **gpt-5.6-luna at xhigh** via codex: measured 2026-07-14 it matched or beat sonnet on recall (100% on exhaustive enumerations, tied deep traces, won routine-chore quality) with zero false cites, at ~3x speed and ~80% lower cost. Exhaustive enumerations carry a cross-model verify pass ‚Äî luna's historical failure mode is a confident wrong count, and a luna/sonnet/sol verifier caught 100% of planted undercounts in testing. Route recon to **sonnet** when the surface is Claude-only (the `Explore` hook, or anywhere codex isn't reachable), when coverage must sweep >300K tokens in one pass (luna's retrieval cliff ‚Äî xhigh does not move it), or when a silent miss is unrecoverable and no verify pass will run. Recon-grade luna is `xhigh` only ‚Äî its `high` tier drops whole subsystems on deep traces. Pass `model: sonnet` to `Explore` ‚Äî it silently defaults to haiku. |
| haiku-4.5 | 10 | 2 | 1 | Single-fact mechanical steps only (classify/label one thing per item). |
| gpt-5.6-sol | 3 | 8 | 5 | Via the codex skill: code/diff review (finder and refuter passes), security review/audit and verification of security-sensitive code (auth, input validation, crypto, secrets ‚Äî implementing that code stays in the fable lane), bug diagnosis, and **the default implementation lane for bounded, decision-light changes to existing code** ‚Äî the plan, work order, or repeated pattern has already made the decisions and what remains is execution against code that already exists: scoped edits, well-specified small features, bug fixes, terminal/shell-heavy execution, and repetitive bounded sweeps (test conversions, migrations, mechanical refactors at scale) as parallel codex-wrapper/codex-ask fan-outs from a workflow. **Large amounts of net-new code stay on opus ‚Äî fable if the surface is very crucial**: sol is markedly stronger at modifying existing code than at authoring a large new subsystem from scratch (measured 2026-07-14 it matched opus on bounded existing-code briefs but failed to converge within a 45-minute cap on decision-dense refactors that opus completed; a well-structured net-new feature it still handled fine, so the line is *large or decision-dense* net-new, not all net-new). **Economics: sol is not cheaper than opus** ‚Äî input matches opus ($5/MTok) and output costs ~20% more ($30 vs $25/MTok); its only edge is task-level token efficiency on bounded work, which inverts on scope drift or non-convergence. Two weaknesses gate the lane: sol's calls degrade as mid-task decisions pile up ‚Äî decision-dense or exploratory work stays on opus even when bounded ‚Äî and it matches surrounding code conventions less faithfully, which the standard finder/refuter review plus capt-hook guards backstop (lean sol harder where capt-hook coverage is live; keep opus where it's thin). Also: plateau second opinions, imagegen, rote throwaway scripts (gpt-5.6-luna may take the rote/bulk lane at the agent's discretion, and now carries the recon lane at `xhigh` ‚Äî see the sonnet-5 row; luna's `high` tier drops whole subsystems on deep traces; `service_tier=fast` stays pinned on every variant). Sol runs `xhigh` only ‚Äî at `max` its first-token latency inverts to 131s vs opus's 40s, so `max` is not a sol escalation rung; a hard miss escalates to fable, and ultra execution mode (exposed since codex 0.144.0) is not a retry rung either: worst-in-class scope discipline at 3‚Äì5x cost. From workflows/subagents, `model` takes only Claude models ‚Äî spawn the `codex:codex-wrapper` agent (ships with the codex plugin) with the self-contained question as its prompt; or invoke `Skill(codex)` directly (inline since codex plugin 0.10.0). |

These are defaults with standing permission to escalate any agent whose output misses the bar ‚Äî escalation means fable; judge the output, not the price tag. Route by task type ‚Äî context-window pressure is not a routing cue. `general-purpose`/`Plan` subagents inherit the session model; pass `model` whenever the table disagrees. The table binds nested spawns too: a subagent or teammate spawning implementers routes them exactly as the orchestrator would (measured 2026-07-14: eleven bounded test-module conversions all went to nested opus agents while the sol lane sat unused).

**Check back on the unexpected.** Delegated agents never improvise a detour. When something unexpected changes the task's shape ‚Äî a scope surprise, an invalidated assumption, a plan that doesn't survive contact with the code, a task that turns out different than described ‚Äî the agent stops and returns early with findings plus 2-4 concrete options; the fable orchestrator picks the next step (AGENTS.md ¬ß Ask Before Assuming, one level up). Transient friction is not shape-change: retries, rate limits, and external failures stay autonomous per AGENTS.md ¬ß General Rules. Deciding next steps is never delegated downward ‚Äî a wrapper doesn't ask codex/sonnet what to do next. Claude subagents and workflow agents inherit this file; gpt-5.6 does not, so codex prompts carry the contract (the codex skill and codex-wrapper agent embed it).

**Delegated reports are narration ‚Äî the disk is the record.** A delegated agent's final message is a lossy, discipline-dependent channel; the deliverable lives at a caller-chosen disk address. A codex fan-out mints its root and lane roster with `codex-ask --mint-root <lane>...` (scratchpad-adopting; never hand-mint scratch ‚Äî no repo-relative dirs, no `.claude/scratch`, no `~/.claude`), passes each agent its lane via `-s "$ROOT/<lane>"` (a pre-created lane that never ran is a visible `no-run`, not absent), and ends with a deterministic `codex-ask --collect "$ROOT"` stage the gate consumes: it classifies each lane from disk alone (`no-run`/`pending`/`running`/`died`/`completed`/`failed`) as one JSONL record. A lane reported "failed" whose record is `completed` is paperwork ‚Äî recover its `reply_file`, never re-dispatch; a lane reported "succeeded" that reads `no-run` never ran. Implementation lanes also reconcile the tree against the lane's expected fileset (a shared working copy makes the tree truth about the world, not attribution) ‚Äî green on an untouched tree is vacuous. Redo only `no-run`, `died`, and genuinely-failed lanes. Lanes end at the working tree ‚Äî **codex edits, Claude ships**: the commit and push run on a native Claude agent (`ccx vcs ship`, after reconciliation), never inside a codex lane ‚Äî jj and bookmark discipline, the prek hook gate, and shared-working-copy safety all live on the Claude side.

**Returns:** short verdict lanes carry a `{status, summary}` micro-schema ‚Äî enforce it natively with `codex-ask --schema <file>` (‚Üí codex `--output-schema`), where structure IS the answer and the session is too short to risk a retry cap. Implementation lanes return schema-free prose led by their `REPLY_FILE:` pointer line ‚Äî a schema after a long session is how the StructuredOutput retry cap nulls finished work for information the disk already holds. Background and named agents deliver via `SendMessage` as their last action ‚Äî bare final text from them is not delivered (a harness boundary, not protocol coverage); an idle agent with no result gets one nudge before any redo.

**Effort**: `xhigh` by default ‚Äî fleet `settings.json` pins it as of 2026-07-14 (the old `max` pin made thinking roughly half of every implementing subagent's output); fable implementation may run `high`. `max` only after an xhigh attempt falls short. Verification runs at the same or higher model + effort tier than the work it checks; lanes the Models table routes to gpt-5.6-sol (refuter passes, security review/audit, verification of security-sensitive code) count as same-tier at `xhigh`, and sol-implemented code takes the same finder/refuter review as any other contributor's ‚Äî escalation and findings synthesis stay on fable. The tier rule bites on un-routed choices: never verify opus work with sonnet.

**Phase intermediates may be broken.** In a phased plan, only the final state must be coherent. Shims, dual-mode params, and interphase adapters exist to be deleted next phase ‚Äî skip them.

**Authoring requirement**: every plan must include the `## Workflow Plan` section described in AGENTS.md `## Writing Plans`. A plan without it is incomplete.

**Reusable orchestrations**: save repeatable runs to `.claude/workflows/`; they become `/` commands.
